subject: AP Physics 1
custom_notes: Reviewed Unit 3 Work, Energy, and Power bank extracted from combined Unit 3-4 source.

questionTypes.type: Calculate translational kinetic energy from mass and velocity

questions.question: A 4.0 kg cart travels at a constant speed of 5.0 m/s. What is the translational kinetic energy of the cart?
Which of the following is the correct expression for the kinetic energy of an object of mass M moving with speed V?
An object with mass 0.5 kg has a kinetic energy of 16 J. What is the speed of the object?
If two objects have the same mass but object A is moving three times as fast as object B, how does the kinetic energy of A compare to B?

questionTypes.type: Predict change in kinetic energy from velocity scaling

questions.question: If the speed of an object is doubled while its mass is kept constant, its kinetic energy increases by a factor of:
An object's speed is reduced to one-third of its original value. By what factor does its kinetic energy change?
A student doubles the mass of a cart but reduces its speed by half. The new kinetic energy is what fraction of the original?
To quadruple the kinetic energy of an object without changing its mass, the speed must be increased by a factor of:

questionTypes.type: Calculate work done by a constant force at an angle

questions.question: A 50 N force is applied at an angle of 60 degrees to the horizontal to pull a crate 10 m across a floor. How much work is done by the force?
A block is pulled 5 m horizontally by a 20 N force acting at 37 degrees above the horizontal. Given cos(37) = 0.8, what is the work done?
Work is defined as the product of displacement and which component of the applied force?
A box is pushed 4 m across a floor by a constant force F applied at an angle theta. If the work done is F*d, what is the angle theta?

questionTypes.type: Distinguish between zero and non-zero work

questions.question: In which of the following cases is the net work done on an object equal to zero?
options: A block sliding at constant velocity
questions.correct_option: A block sliding at constant velocity
questions.answer: C. A block sliding at constant velocity

questions.question: A person holds a heavy box at arm's length for 60 seconds without moving it. How much work is done on the box by the person?
Which of the following forces does ZERO work on a block being pulled horizontally along a table?
An object moves in a circular path at constant speed. The work done by the centripetal force is:

questionTypes.type: Calculate gravitational potential energy for vertical lifts

questions.question: A student lifts a 5 kg box vertically 0.40 m. How much work is done by the student?
If an object is lifted to three times its original height, its gravitational potential energy relative to the ground increases by a factor of:
A 2 kg book is moved from a shelf 1 m high to a shelf 2.5 m high. What is the change in its gravitational potential energy?
The choice of the 'zero point' for gravitational potential energy is:
options: Always the center of the Earth
Always the lowest point in the problem
Arbitrary and depends on the observer
questions.correct_option: Arbitrary and depends on the observer
questions.answer: C. Arbitrary and depends on the observer

questionTypes.type: Predict speed from conservation of mechanical energy

questions.question: A cart rolls from rest down a frictionless incline from a height H. What is its speed at the bottom?
If a cart is released from height H and reaches speed V at the bottom, from what height must it be released to reach speed 2V?
When a cart rolls halfway down a frictionless incline (height H/2), its speed is what fraction of its speed at the bottom (height 0)?
An object is thrown upward with kinetic energy K. At the highest point of its flight, its kinetic energy is zero. What happened to that energy?
questions.answer: B. It turned into potential energy

questionTypes.type: Determine work done from area under force-displacement graph

questions.question: A force vs. position graph shows a triangle with a base of 0.03 m and a peak force of 50 N. What is the work done?
The area under a force-displacement graph represents which physical quantity?
A graph of force vs. position for a non-linear spring is given. To find the change in kinetic energy of a mass attached to it, a student should:
options: Find the area under the curve
Take the average of the endpoints
questions.correct_option: Find the area under the curve
questions.answer: B. Find the area under the curve

questions.question: On an AP Physics graph of force vs. position, if the units of force are Newtons and position are centimeters, what must be done to the area to get Joules?

questionTypes.type: Solve for spring potential energy using displacement

questions.question: A spring with force constant 4 N/m is stretched 0.10 m. What is the elastic potential energy stored in the spring?
If the compression of a spring is tripled, the elastic potential energy stored in it increases by a factor of:
A 100 N/m spring is compressed 0.2 m. How much kinetic energy will a 1 kg block have when released at the equilibrium position?
The potential energy of an ideal spring is proportional to:
options: The square of the displacement x^2
The square root of the displacement
questions.correct_option: The square of the displacement x^2
questions.answer: B. The square of the displacement x^2

questionTypes.type: Distinguish between conservative and nonconservative forces

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a conservative force?
The work done by which type of force is path-dependent?
If a system returns to its original configuration and the net work done by a force is zero, that force is:
Thermal energy is typically produced by the work of which type of force?

questionTypes.type: Calculate power delivered by a constant force

questions.question: A motor pulls a block with a constant force of 100 N at a constant speed of 2 m/s. What is the power delivered by the motor?
Power is defined as:
Student A does 20 J of work in 2 s. Student B does 20 J of work in 1 s. Which student develops more power?
A constant force F moves an object a distance D in time T. If the time is doubled for the same force and distance, the power is:

questionTypes.type: Analyze energy transformation with nonconservative forces

questions.question: A block slides down a rough incline. Which of the following is true about the mechanical energy of the block-Earth system?
The total energy of a system is always conserved. If mechanical energy decreases, where did the energy go?
questions.answer: B. It turned into internal/thermal energy

questions.question: A student finds that the work done by friction on a block is equal to 10 J. This means the mechanical energy of the system:
In the presence of air resistance, a falling ball's kinetic energy at the bottom is:

questionTypes.type: Determine total mechanical energy for mass-spring systems

questions.question: In a horizontal mass-spring oscillator with no friction, the total mechanical energy is:
At maximum displacement, the mechanical energy of a spring system is entirely:
If a mass-spring system has total energy E, its kinetic energy at equilibrium is:
Doubling the amplitude of a spring oscillator changes the total mechanical energy by what factor?

questionTypes.type: Predict changes in height using conservation of energy scaling

questions.question: A ball thrown upward with speed v reaches height H. If it is thrown with speed 3v, it will reach height:
To double the maximum height of a projectile, the initial launch speed must be increased by a factor of:
A roller coaster starts from height H and reaches speed V at the bottom. To reach speed 1.5V, it must start from height:
If the mass of a projectile is doubled but its launch speed is the same, the maximum height reached will:

questionTypes.type: Analyze energy bar charts for closed systems

questions.question: In an energy bar chart for a ball falling through the air, which bar must decrease as the ball falls?
For a system where friction is present, the final 'total energy' bar must be _____ the initial 'total energy' bar.
If the kinetic energy bar (K) increases from 2 units to 10 units and potential energy (U) decreases from 10 units to 2 units, the net work by nonconservative forces was:
Which bar chart represents an object at rest at the top of a hill?

questionTypes.type: Calculate potential energy in gravitational fields (inverse distance)

questions.question: The general equation for gravitational potential energy between two planets is Ug = -G*M1*M2/d. If the distance d is doubled, the potential energy:
If the distance between two masses is doubled, the MAGNITUDE of the gravitational potential energy:
Gravitational potential energy between two large objects is zero when the distance d is:
If both masses are doubled and the distance between them is doubled, how does the potential energy change?

questionTypes.type: Design experiments to determine spring constant from energy

questions.question: To determine the spring constant k using energy conservation, a student should measure the compression distance and:
options: The final speed of a launched mass
The total length of the spring
questions.correct_option: The final speed of a launched mass
questions.answer: A. The final speed of a launched mass

questions.question: Which graph will allow a student to determine 'k' as the slope if they measure elastic potential energy (U) and displacement (x)?
In an experiment where a spring launches a ball vertically, which of the following is sufficient to find 'k'?
options: Mass, max height, and spring compression
questions.correct_option: Mass, max height, and spring compression
questions.answer: B. Mass, max height, and spring compression

questions.question: To reduce uncertainty in a work-energy lab, a student should:
options: Use a spring with a very small k
Perform multiple trials and average the data
Use a stopwatch instead of a motion detector
questions.correct_option: Perform multiple trials and average the data
questions.answer: C. Perform multiple trials and average the data

questionTypes.type: Relate mechanical energy change to external work

questions.question: The total mechanical energy of a system changes if and only if:
options: External work is done on the system
questions.correct_option: External work is done on the system
questions.answer: B. External work is done on the system

questions.question: If 50 J of positive work is done on a system and its potential energy increases by 30 J, how does the kinetic energy change?
Work done BY a system on its surroundings results in mechanical energy that:
A student pushes a box across a floor at constant speed. The work done by the student:
options: Equals the increase in internal energy due to friction
Equals the increase in kinetic energy
Equals the increase in potential energy
questions.correct_option: Equals the increase in internal energy due to friction
questions.answer: B. Equals the increase in internal energy due to friction

questionTypes.type: Solve for instantaneous speed at points of interest using energy

questions.question: A mass m on a spring with constant k is released from rest at displacement A. What is its speed when it is at the equilibrium position?
A pendulum is released from height H. At what height is its kinetic energy equal to its potential energy (relative to the bottom)?
A cart moving at speed v hits a spring and compresses it a distance x. If it moves at 2v, the compression will be:
In a frictionless system, the speed of an object at any height y is determined by its initial speed v0 and initial height y0 by:

questionTypes.type: Determine net work from change in kinetic energy

questions.question: A block initially at rest is subjected to a net work of 50 J. What is the final kinetic energy of the block?
If the kinetic energy of a cart increases from 10 J to 30 J, the net work done on it is:
A car's kinetic energy changes from K to K/4 as it brakes. The net work done by the brakes is:
The Work-Energy Theorem states that net work equals the change in:

questionTypes.type: Compare power developed by different agents performing same work

questions.question: Student A lifts a 50-N box 40 cm in 2 s. Student B lifts a 40-N box 50 cm in 1 s. Which student does more work?
questions.answer: C. They do the same work

questions.question: In the scenario from the previous question, which student develops more power?
options: Student A because the force is larger

questions.question: A machine does work W in time T. A second machine does work 2W in time 2T. How do their power ratings compare?
If you climb a flight of stairs in half the time it took your friend, but you both have the same mass, your power was:
